title: Motherboy Xxx

jokes.text: Lucille is already dressed, made up and waiting for Michael at an ungodly hour — narrator notes 'which put her wake-up time in the 3:00, 3:30 area'
jokes.setup: Narrator says Lucille was already dressed, made up and waiting for Michael who got up early to avoid his family
jokes.punchline: The narrator deadpans that this put her wake-up time 'in the 3:00, 3:30 area' — implying Lucille's obsessive vanity requires hours of preparation
jokes.explanation: The narrator's clinical precision ('3:00, 3:30 area') is the joke — it's delivered like a weather report for something pathological. Perfect character integration: this is completely specific to Lucille's vanity and controlling nature. Economy is flawless — one sentence does enormous work characterizing her obsession.

jokes.text: 'As one entered sexual maturity, and the other one left it, it became harder to win.'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains Lucille and Buster have won 'Cutest Couple' at Motherboy multiple times
jokes.punchline: Narrator notes that as Buster aged (entered sexual maturity) and Lucille aged (left it), the incestuous optics of the contest became harder to win — the joke being that this was EVER acceptable, and the reason they stopped winning is darkly absurd
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy: the narrator treats the incestuous Cutest Couple dynamic as a logistical problem — 'it became harder to win' — rather than moral horror. The clinical framing amplifies the wrongness. The specific detail about which direction each person's sexual maturity was traveling is elegant and deeply uncomfortable. One of the show's signature moves executed perfectly.

jokes.text: 'I'm so hot.' / 'If you were hot, Mother, we would win.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says 'I'm so hot' meaning she's feeling the heat / is excited
jokes.punchline: Michael interprets it literally as attractive — and then applies it as the reason they'd win a Cutest Couple contest, which is its own layer of disturbing
jokes.explanation: Good quick wordplay with an added layer: Michael's response is functionally an assessment of his mother's attractiveness in the context of a mother-son beauty pageant, which is its own cringe. Works on two levels but neither is exceptional.

jokes.text: 'It's not just Motherboy. It's Motherboy XXX!'
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to get Michael excited about Motherboy
jokes.punchline: 'XXX' added to the name of a mother-son dance event carries unmistakably pornographic connotations, played totally straight by Lucille
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the naming — the show is aware of the connotation and deploys it with complete earnestness from Lucille. Also connects to the episode title. The irony is that this is also the 30th anniversary of the event (XXX = 30), giving it a plausible innocent reading, which makes the double meaning sharper.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That could have something to do with the fact that a seal ate his hand.' / Lucille: 'I don't know what it is.' / Michael: 'I think that's what it is.' / Lucille: 'Who knows?' / Narrator: 'It was that.'
jokes.setup: Discussion of why Buster is mopey
jokes.punchline: The narrator's final 'It was that' punctuates Lucille's determined denial with absolute authority
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in comedic structure: the back-and-forth escalates with Michael's increasingly obvious explanation being stonewalled by Lucille's denial, then the narrator closes it with two words — 'It was that.' — that demolish Lucille's obliviousness with surgical efficiency. It's the show's narrator device at its best: used not just for exposition but as a straight-man punchline. Exceptional economy.

jokes.text: Buster seeing reminders of the accident everywhere — gasping and screaming at innocuous objects — visual gag montage
jokes.setup: Narrator says Buster became haunted by the loss, seeing reminders of the accident everywhere
jokes.punchline: Buster gasps and screams at everyday objects that resemble a hand or hook (implied from context and the repeated [Gasps, Screams] stage directions)
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works on character — Buster's trauma is played for maximum absurdity. The repeated gasping establishes a running gag. Rewatch value because you see the specific objects he's reacting to. The transcript gives only the audio cues but the visual execution is clearly designed to be the joke.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Mother, I can't sleep. I may not be able to go to dance practice today.'
jokes.setup: Buster has been screaming in horror at phantom reminders of his lost hand
jokes.punchline: His complaint to his mother is delivered with the gravity of a Victorian invalid — 'I may not be able to go to dance practice today' — as if the concern is dance practice, not the PTSD
jokes.explanation: Pure Buster: the fact that he's reporting to his mother like a child, and his concern is dance practice rather than his shattered psyche, is a perfect character beat. Tony Hale's delivery of lines like this is crucial — the character's child-like dependency on Lucille makes even mundane complaints funny.

jokes.text: 'I mean, look how he zips now.'
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Michael to zip her up — and Michael is criticizing Lucille for not supporting Buster after his amputation
jokes.punchline: Michael points out that Buster now zips differently because he only has one hand — a simple, devastating observation delivered casually
jokes.explanation: The joke lands because it's specific and physically concrete — not 'Buster has trouble doing things' but the precise detail of zipping. The one-handed zipping in the moment of Lucille being zipped up creates a bitter irony. Economy is the strong suit: four words encapsulate Buster's disability and Lucille's negligence simultaneously.

jokes.text: The Bluth children had been uneasy about missing limbs since their father used a one-armed man to teach them lessons. ['That's why you don't yell.'] / [All Whimpering]
jokes.setup: Michael accuses Lucille of being uncomfortable around Buster's missing hand
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals — via flashback — that George Sr. traumatized all his children by using a one-armed man as an object lesson, explaining their collective phobia about missing limbs
jokes.explanation: This is a multi-season callback to J. Walter Weatherman, the one-armed man George Sr. hired to teach the children lessons. The revelation that this is why ALL the Bluth kids are weird about amputees recontextualizes the current plot beautifully. 'That's why you don't yell' is the punchline to a lesson-teaching scene shown in flashback. High rewatch value because it deepens both the past and present jokes.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'm so glad there wasn't one of those for daddies and daughters.' / Michael: 'Of course they have father-daughter dances.' / Lindsay: 'They do? He never took me?' / Lucille: 'It was before we did your nose. Toodle-oo.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay expresses relief that there's no equivalent incestuous father-daughter dance
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually reveals that Lindsay was excluded from father-daughter dances because of her nose — before her nose job — delivered with complete callousness and a cheerful 'Toodle-oo'
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy: Lucille's cruelty is so casual and complete that it's shocking. She doesn't even register what she's said as hurtful — 'Toodle-oo' is the perfect punctuation. Three things happen at once: (1) Lindsay learns her father never took her to dances, (2) she learns it was because of her appearance, (3) Lucille exits without apology. The 'Toodle-oo' elevates it from great to near-perfect. High quotability, high rewatch.

jokes.text: 'You're up awful early.' / 'Well, my marriage is falling apart. Tobias is back with Carl.'
jokes.setup: Michael is surprised Lindsay is awake early
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's reason for being up is her marriage troubles — and her framing of 'Tobias is back with Carl' sounds like a romantic rival situation, when Carl is his acting teacher
jokes.explanation: Mild misdirection — 'back with Carl' sounds romantic, but Carl is Carl Weathers. Functional but not exceptional; the joke is the framing rather than a real twist.

jokes.text: Tobias's vanity plate for being cast as 'Frightened Inmate Number Two' — and the narrator notes 'this wasn't the first time'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says Tobias said the same thing (about buying her happiness) when he got cast as Frightened Inmate Number Two
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals he got a vanity plate made for the achievement — implying something absurd like 'FRGHTNDINMATE2' — and then adds 'this wasn't the first time,' implying he's had MULTIPLE plates for bit roles
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of the vanity plate (presumably reading something like 'FRGHTNDINMATE2') is so specific it's brilliant — Tobias is so delusional about his career that he celebrates a non-speaking bit role with a custom license plate. 'This wasn't the first time' adds a devastating implication: there are previous plates from previous non-roles. This is exceptional character integration — completely specific to Tobias's self-delusion.

jokes.text: George Sr. is secretly living in the attic — narrator reveals this while George Sr. tries to be friendly to passing family members: 'Nellie. Now — who wants to take their top off?'
jokes.setup: Narrator says George Sr. was secretly living in the attic above them and was starved for company
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s opener to his grandchildren is 'Now — who wants to take their top off?' — his idea of socializing is immediately inappropriate
jokes.explanation: The joke works because George Sr.'s social isolation has curdled his social skills. 'Who wants to take their top off?' is the kind of thing a man who's been living in an attic for months thinks is normal small talk. Character-specific and darkly funny.

jokes.text: 'Hey, there's booze in this.' (about the 'Promise Land' canteen Ann has given George Michael)
jokes.setup: George Michael explains the Promise Land camping trip is about making promises as a tribute to generations that preceded you — a serious Christian event
jokes.punchline: George Sr. finds booze in the canteen, immediately undercutting the Christian framing
jokes.explanation: Mild subversion of the Christian camp setup — funny but not particularly inventive. The irony is clean and quick.

jokes.text: 'That's the Promise Land that Ann's taking you to? You're gonna need this more than Polly here.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. hears the Promise Land has booze and sees it differently than its religious intent
jokes.punchline: He offers George Michael the booze — also calling the bird 'Polly' which connects to his habit of calling his parrots by names he'll later confuse (Nellie/Polly)
jokes.explanation: George Sr. offering his teenage grandson booze for a Christian camping trip is funny but plays more as character color than a crafted joke.

jokes.text: 'I had to take all pumps out of here a long time ago.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby ask for the pump for an air mattress
jokes.punchline: George Sr. cryptically says he had to take all pumps out 'a long time ago' — implying some dire past incident, but delivered utterly casually
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan: the casual delivery of something that implies past catastrophe. What happened? Why no pumps? We never learn, which is the joke. Very Arrested Development in structure.

jokes.text: George Sr. preaches against fidelity and pledging yourself to a woman: 'I wine 'em and dine 'em, but I don't let 'em tell me what to do. I don't... let 'em tell me... what to do.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is railing against George Michael going to the Promise Land and making pledges of fidelity
jokes.punchline: His speech about not letting women tell him what to do trails off into an increasingly unconvincing repetition — as if he's trying to convince himself — while he's hiding in his wife's attic
jokes.explanation: The irony is perfect and layered: George Sr. is delivering a speech about masculine independence and not being controlled by women WHILE hiding in his wife's attic. The trailing repetition ('I don't... let 'em tell me... what to do') signals he doesn't believe his own words. The gap between his posture and his reality is the joke, and it's rich.

jokes.text: 'Okay, I should've never taken the pumps out of here.'
jokes.setup: George Sr.'s speech about independence caused him to hyperventilate or have some reaction requiring the pump
jokes.punchline: He mutters the callback to his pump explanation, implying his bravado literally caused a medical need for the pump he removed
jokes.explanation: This is the callback to the pump joke, and it lands well — the callback rewards attention and retroactively gives the pump joke a punchline it didn't have. Structurally tight: setup earlier, callback immediately after the joke that needed it.

jokes.text: Michael says 'I respect my son's choices' and immediately follows with 'George Michael, I don't think you should be going on this Promise Land thing.'
jokes.setup: Michael lectures his father about respecting choices
jokes.punchline: He immediately overrides his son's choice in the same breath
jokes.explanation: Classic hypocrisy joke with clean structure — the irony is immediate, with Michael contradicting himself within seconds. Works because it's an ongoing character trait: Michael always positions himself as the reasonable one while behaving identically to the people he criticizes.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'What? Why? Because I'd miss school?' / Michael: 'No.' / George Michael: 'You get to miss school for that?' / Narrator: 'And that's when Maeby decided to become a devout Christian.'
jokes.setup: Michael says George Michael shouldn't go to the Promise Land — George Michael assumes it's about missing school
jokes.punchline: The revelation that you get to MISS SCHOOL for it instantly converts Maeby to Christianity — narrator delivers it with clinical finality
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural beat: George Michael's aside ('You get to miss school for that?') sets up Maeby's conversion. The narrator delivering 'And that's when Maeby decided to become a devout Christian' as though it's a perfectly logical outcome is a great use of the narrator as punchline. Character-specific for Maeby, who is consistently opportunistic.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Do you guys know where I could get one of those gold necklaces with the "T" on it?' / Michael: 'That's a cross.' / Maeby: 'Across from where?'
jokes.setup: Maeby has instantly converted to Christianity and is asking about Christian symbols
jokes.punchline: 'Across from where?' — a perfect malapropism/pun that sounds like ignorance but may be feigned
jokes.explanation: 'Across from where?' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode — a perfectly constructed wordplay joke that's also ambiguous: is Maeby genuinely confused or deliberately deflecting? The three-word punchline is textbook economy. High quotability.

jokes.text: Gob had once gotten married on a dare — a fact he'd only remembered when he was served with divorce papers.
jokes.setup: Gob asks Barry about his divorce
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the marriage existed only as a dare and Gob had FORGOTTEN about it until divorce papers arrived
jokes.explanation: Character-perfect: this is only funny because it's Gob. The detail of 'only remembered when served with divorce papers' is the perfect escalation — he didn't just forget the marriage existed, he forgot it UNTIL legal action reminded him. Deeply specific to Gob's chaos.

jokes.text: 'What about her cans? She have big cans?' / 'You don't remember her at all?' / 'It was one night of wild passion.' / 'And yet you didn't notice her body.' / 'I like to look in the mirror.'
jokes.setup: It's established Gob doesn't remember his wife's appearance
jokes.punchline: 'I like to look in the mirror' — Gob's vanity and narcissism explain why he didn't notice his wife during their supposed night of passion: he was looking at himself
jokes.explanation: 'I like to look in the mirror' is one of the best Gob lines in the episode — it's a perfect character reveal disguised as a throwaway. It explains everything about Gob (narcissism, self-obsession, inability to connect with others) in five words. The escalating exchange that leads there has perfect structure. Highly quotable and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: 'That would be disgusting if you'd actually slept with her, but I don't think you did.' / 'I did! And it was disgusting.' / Narrator: 'They didn't, but it would have been.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Gob didn't actually consummate the marriage
jokes.punchline: Gob insists he did and that it was disgusting — the narrator authoritatively corrects both of them: 'They didn't, but it would have been' — agreeing with Gob's characterization of hypothetical disgust while correcting the facts
jokes.explanation: The narrator here is working overtime: 'They didn't, but it would have been' is a remarkable construction — it fact-checks Gob while agreeing with his value judgment. The narrator treating Gob's wife as inherently disgusting is dark, and the deadpan delivery makes it funnier. Narratorial authority as comedic device at its best.

jokes.text: 'And that's not even including Buster's Swatch.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains the seal is worth $250,000 according to his wife's lawyers
jokes.punchline: He adds 'and that's not even including Buster's Swatch' — treating his brother's severed hand as merely a vessel for a cheap watch claim
jokes.explanation: The 'Buster's Swatch' joke is one of the episode's best: the mention of a Swatch (a cheap, colorful watch) as a legal claim alongside a $250K seal is perfectly absurd. It implies Buster was wearing a cheap watch at the time of the attack, that Gob thinks this is worth noting in legal discussions, and that the watch is somehow still in the seal. Layers of dark absurdity packed into one throwaway line. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Barry: 'The seal? Must've had a tracking device on it. Someone tracked it all the way to a dock in Dana Point.'
jokes.setup: The plot complication of finding the seal that ate Buster's hand
jokes.punchline: Barry has somehow obtained tracking data on the seal — the absurdity of a lawyer casually tracking a seal that ate a client's hand is treated with total normality
jokes.explanation: Plot-functional absurdism. The tracking device on the seal is a necessary plot device delivered with some absurdist flavor, but it's mostly setup for later jokes.

jokes.text: 'At the very least, we can get Buster's Swatch back, right? Up top. No?'
jokes.setup: Discussion of potentially finding the seal and retrieving Buster's hand for a transplant
jokes.punchline: Gob's priority is the Swatch — he tries to get a high-five for this insight and gets nothing ('No?')
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Swatch joke lands harder than the original because Gob's fixation on the Swatch over his brother's hand is the joke, and 'Up top. No?' adds a layer of social obliviousness. Gob seeking approval for this grotesque prioritization and being rebuffed is perfect. The failed high-five is a visual beat that works in audio too.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers to Tobias at Burger King: 'Thanks for meeting me down here at Burger King. I'm tryin' to get them to underwrite a new TV project I'm workin' on. Get some money in exchange for settin' a scene here in Burger King. As long as you don't draw attention to it.'
jokes.setup: Tobias meets Carl Weathers at Burger King
jokes.punchline: Carl explains he's trying to get Burger King to underwrite his project by setting scenes there — while saying 'don't draw attention to it' — which is exactly what he's doing by naming Burger King repeatedly
jokes.explanation: A meta joke about product placement — Carl is essentially doing exactly the thing he's saying not to do. The show is satirizing the mechanics of branded content while doing it. Self-aware and well-executed.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I could not betray my family like that.' / Carl: 'Come on, man. I've got every part cast except for George Sr.' / Tobias: 'I want that part.' / Carl: 'Then sign over the rights.'
jokes.setup: Tobias refuses to sign a release betraying his family
jokes.punchline: The moment he hears there's a juicy acting part, his principles dissolve instantly
jokes.explanation: A clean hypocrisy beat — Tobias's acting ambition overrides his stated values in real time. Good character work, not exceptional craft.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'Did you know that you can get a refill on any drink you want here, and it's free?' / [Laughs] / Narrator [chuckling]: 'It sure is.'
jokes.setup: Carl goes to get a soda and discovers free refills at Burger King
jokes.punchline: Carl is inexplicably delighted by free refills, and the NARRATOR laughs and agrees ('It sure is') — the narrator breaking character to comment on Burger King is meta and absurd
jokes.explanation: The joke is the narrator being bribed along with everyone else — Ron Howard's narrator chuckling and saying 'It sure is' about Burger King's refill policy is a brilliant meta moment. It implies the show itself is in on the product placement joke. Carl's genuine delight at free refills despite being a working actor is absurdist character comedy. The narrator breaking its neutral omniscient voice is rare and effective.

jokes.text: George Michael playing checkers with Buster — 'King me.' / [Gasps] / 'Oh, God! I keep forgetting about this.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Buster are playing checkers
jokes.punchline: Buster gasps at 'King me' — presumably because kinged checkers pieces are stacked in a way that resembles a hand gesture or fist — and he reacts as though reminded of his missing hand
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Buster's phantom-hand trauma gag — 'King me' triggering a gasp is a perfectly chosen trigger because it's a pointing gesture. The quick admission 'Oh God! I keep forgetting about this' is tragicomic: he keeps forgetting he has no hand, which is itself a form of comic pathos. Rewatch bonus because you see what the stacked checker looks like.

jokes.text: George Michael asks if Buster offered a bowl of soup; Lucille asks 'Can it open a can?' — referring to Buster's hook — and both Buster and Lucille react with horror: 'Oh, God!'
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Buster if he offered George Michael soup — George Michael says 'Can it open a can?' meaning 'can he open a can'
jokes.punchline: Lucille takes 'can it' as referring to Buster's hook/stump — 'Can it open a can?' — and both she and Buster react with horror
jokes.explanation: Nice double-meaning wordplay using Buster's disability — 'Can it open a can?' is simultaneously innocent and horrifying. The shared reaction of horror from Lucille AND Buster (the person asking the question AND the person being asked about) is a good comedic beat.

jokes.text: 'Aren't you the sweetest thing, spending some time with what's left of your uncle.'
jokes.setup: Lucille observes George Michael visiting Buster
jokes.punchline: 'What's left of your uncle' — Lucille describes Buster as a remnant of a person, entirely casually, with sweet maternal approval in her voice
jokes.explanation: Lucille at her most casually monstrous. The sweetness of the delivery against 'what's left of your uncle' is where the joke lives — she means it as a compliment and doesn't register the dehumanization. Highly quotable, excellent character integration for Lucille specifically.

jokes.text: 'Aren't you the sweetest thing, spending some time with what's left of your uncle.'
jokes.setup: Lucille observes George Michael's visit to Buster
jokes.punchline: 'What's left of your uncle' — reducing Buster to his physical diminishment with cheerful casualness
jokes.explanation: Peak Lucille — affectionate on the surface, devastating underneath. The casualness of 'what's left of your uncle' is perfectly delivered. Her complete failure to recognize this as cruel is itself character comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: George Michael dressed in a sailor suit by Lucille — who claimed they were going to the Promise Land — being taken to Motherboy instead
jokes.setup: Lucille invites George Michael out of town for the weekend, implying the Promise Land
jokes.punchline: The sailor suit Lucille puts him in is the giveaway — he's going to Motherboy as Buster's replacement, dressed exactly as Buster would be dressed
jokes.explanation: The visual gag of the sailor suit is the reveal — Lucille has essentially replaced Buster with George Michael wholesale, dressing him in Buster's costume. The narrator's 'No, he was going south' / 'South to Motherboy' is a perfectly timed structural reveal. High rewatch value because the sailor suit visual is the joke.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'No, he was going south.' / [pause] / 'South to Motherboy.'
jokes.setup: 'Going south' as a geographic direction and as an idiom for things going wrong
jokes.punchline: The narrator clarifies — 'South to Motherboy' — using a double meaning where 'going south' signals bad news, confirmed by the destination being Motherboy
jokes.explanation: The narrator pause between 'south' and the reveal is a masterful structural beat — it lets the idiom land before clarifying the literal meaning. The two-word punchline 'South to Motherboy' does everything it needs to do.

jokes.text: Buster arrives at the dock by crashing through the car window — Gob: 'Or we could make sure it's the right seal first? I mean, why get his hopes up?'
jokes.setup: Michael wonders if they should call Buster to the dock — then Buster (with his hook) crashes through/onto the car
jokes.punchline: Gob suggests they verify the seal first — 'why get his hopes up?' — implying the hope of a hand reattachment is something to manage, while Buster is already there
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of Buster's arrival combined with Gob's belated sensible suggestion works well. The hook presumably snagging on the window is the visual gag. Gob's reasonable suggestion being completely moot is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster enters with his hook: 'I really hate this stupid hook. But I certainly can't walk around like this.' / Michael/Gob: 'Why not? / Very handsome.'
jokes.setup: Buster expresses self-consciousness about his hook
jokes.punchline: Michael asks 'Why not?' — implying the hook is fine — while Gob says 'Very handsome' — a supportive but absurd assessment of a prosthetic hook
jokes.explanation: A good supporting beat — 'Very handsome' from Gob about a prosthetic hook is absurd in a characteristically Gob way. The double response (genuine Michael vs. absurd Gob) is efficient and funny.

jokes.text: Gob says there's no room for Buster in the car: 'There's not enough room for you and me and the stump and the thing- stuff. Barry!'
jokes.setup: Michael wants Buster to come look for the seal
jokes.punchline: Gob lists the spatial problem: 'you and me and the stump and the thing- stuff' — referring to Buster's stump/hook as luggage, and catching himself ('stuff') — then calling for Barry instead
jokes.explanation: Gob's insensitive reduction of Buster to 'the stump and the thing' before catching himself is dark and specific. The stammer ('stuff') shows a flash of self-awareness before he just calls for Barry anyway. Good Gob characterization.

jokes.text: Tobias practicing his George Sr. voice in the attic: '[Low] I'm George Sr.' / George Sr. appears: 'That's not my voice.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is sneaking around the attic doing a voice he thinks is George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is right there and immediately rejects the impression
jokes.explanation: Good comedic timing — Tobias's self-congratulatory 'I've got the voice' immediately deflated by the real George Sr. The coincidence of George Sr. being present is earned by the premise.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I'm havin' a [bleep] tea party. What does it look like? I live here.'
jokes.setup: Tobias asks what George Sr. is doing in the attic
jokes.punchline: George Sr. says 'I'm having a [bleep] tea party' — the absurdity of a fugitive billionaire having a tea party in an attic — then doubles down: 'I live here'
jokes.explanation: The tea party non-answer is delightfully unhinged — George Sr.'s attic fugitive status makes his territorial 'I live here' deeply absurd. Good energy and delivery implied.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Stop licking my hand, you horse's ass.' [to the parrot]
jokes.setup: Tobias is observing George Sr.'s 'raw power' in the attic
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is addressing his parrot — 'Stop licking my hand, you horse's ass' — with the same aggressive authority he'd use on a person
jokes.explanation: The line works because of context: Tobias sees this as 'raw power' — George Sr. commanding even his parrot with authority. The absurdity of telling a parrot it's a 'horse's ass' is funny, and Tobias mistaking it for menacing masculinity is the comic layer on top.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s condition for Tobias playing him: 'If you play me, you gotta play me like a man and not some mincing little Polly or Nellie.' / 'I get those names confused. Apologies. Apologies all around.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. insists Tobias must play him as a man, not effeminately
jokes.punchline: He uses two slang terms for effeminate men (Polly/Nellie) but confuses which is which — 'I get those names confused' — and the 'Apologies all around' implies he's apologizing to the birds named Polly and Nellie who are present in the attic
jokes.explanation: Dense layering: George Sr. uses slang terms for effeminate men to instruct Tobias (who is himself implied to be closeted) not to play him effeminately, while confusing the terms and then apologizing to the birds he's named Polly and Nellie. 'Apologies all around' is perfect — it acknowledges both potential offenses. Rewatch bonus for catching all the layers.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I would love to be able to say "Look what I've had reattached while you and George Michael were off to Motherboy."'
jokes.setup: Buster hears that Lucille has taken George Michael to Motherboy in his place
jokes.punchline: His reaction is to frame the seal hunt in terms of one-upmanship with his mother — wanting to show off a reattached hand as a counter to her Motherboy trophy
jokes.explanation: Buster's competitive relationship with his mother is perfectly captured here — his motivation for getting his hand reattached is to have a comeback for Lucille. Character-specific and darkly funny.

jokes.text: 'He couldn't wait to swoop in and throw on that sailor suit, because he's not damaged goods.'
jokes.setup: Buster processes that George Michael replaced him at Motherboy
jokes.punchline: 'Because he's not damaged goods' — Buster describes himself as 'damaged goods' entirely matter-of-factly, as if this is an objective market assessment of his value
jokes.explanation: The self-description as 'damaged goods' in the commercial sense (a product with physical defects, reduced in value) is both sad and funny — Buster has internalized his amputee status as a literal reduction in his worth as a Motherboy partner. Highly character-specific to Buster's dependent, self-diminishing psychology.

jokes.text: Barry reveals the tracking device led to a shark, not the seal — 'the shark must've bitten off the part of the seal with the tracking device on it' — and Gob observes: 'So the seal lost his hand too.'
jokes.setup: The team tracks the seal to a dock — but it was a shark
jokes.punchline: The shark ate the tracking device by biting off the seal's flipper — meaning the SEAL also lost its 'hand' (flipper) to the same chain of events
jokes.explanation: The escalating absurdity is excellent: Buster → seal → shark, with each losing a limb in sequence. 'So the seal lost his hand too' is a perfectly delivered observation of this chain. The joke extends to the emotional parallels drawn in the next beat, which elevates it further.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, poor guy's probably out there without a flipper, swimming around in a circle, freaking out his whole family.'
jokes.setup: The seal lost its flipper to the shark
jokes.punchline: Barry describes the seal's predicament with empathy — 'freaking out his whole family' — which mirrors Buster's situation with the Bluths perfectly
jokes.explanation: The parallelism between the seal 'freaking out his whole family' and Buster doing the same is a smart comedic structural choice — it makes the absurdist subplot feel thematically connected. Barry's genuine empathy for the seal is also funny coming from the world's worst lawyer.

jokes.text: 'That tracking device tells me he was raised in captivity. There's no way he stood a chance out in the real world.' / 'See that? He was a victim too.' / 'You both were too sheltered to learn how to protect yourselves.'
jokes.setup: The seal's backstory is being analyzed
jokes.punchline: The analysis of the seal's sheltered upbringing is an exact parallel to Buster's — 'you both were too sheltered to learn how to protect yourselves' collapses the seal and Buster into the same character
jokes.explanation: The explicit parallel between Buster and the seal is one of the episode's most elegantly constructed bits — it takes a running gag (Buster's over-mothering) and maps it onto the seal's literal captivity. The insight 'you both were too sheltered to learn how to protect yourselves' is funny AND thematically resonant with the episode's central conflict about Motherboy. Rewatch: the seal as Buster metaphor deepens on repeat viewings.

jokes.text: Gob: 'And I've got to staple this to a seal. And I've skipped breakfast, so I'm off to Burger King.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Buster decide to rescue George Michael from Motherboy
jokes.punchline: Gob announces he needs to 'staple this to a seal' — completely unexplained — and then pivots to Burger King because he skipped breakfast; the non-sequitur juxtaposition is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'I've got to staple this to a seal' is a perfectly absurdist non-sequitur — we never learn what 'this' is or why it must be stapled to a seal — followed immediately by the mundane concern of breakfast. The Burger King callback lands the product placement joke a second time with exponentially more absurdist effect. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille arrives at the hotel and sees someone else in the exact same color outfit: 'Oh — [bleep] me. The exact same color.' / 'What are the odds?'
jokes.setup: Lucille has dressed George Michael in a specific coordinated outfit for Motherboy
jokes.punchline: Another pair has the exact same outfit colors — Lucille's competitive horror is captured in her reaction
jokes.explanation: A fairly standard fashion-horror beat elevated slightly by Lucille's competitive nature and the context of a mother-son dance event. Not particularly original but lands as a character beat.

jokes.text: 'You think enough time has passed to do Sonny and Cher?' / 'We're here for Motherboy.' / 'And you do realize this is not the band.'
jokes.setup: Lucille considers costume options for Motherboy; Motherboy is also a heavy metal band
jokes.punchline: The narrator clarifies: 'Motherboy was also a heavy metal band that used to rock pretty hard in the '70s. We are legally obligated to make the distinction.' — the show acknowledging an actual real-world band named Motherboy
jokes.explanation: 'We are legally obligated to make the distinction' is a great meta moment — the show breaking the fourth wall to acknowledge a real-world naming conflict. The formal legal language in a narrator joke is very Arrested Development.

jokes.text: The room is put under the name 'Karmopolis.'
jokes.setup: The hotel clerk asks what name to put the room under — Lucille is a fugitive's wife trying to avoid her son finding them
jokes.punchline: 'Karmopolis' — a made-up word that sounds vaguely Greek and is completely nonsensical as a name, used as a pseudonym
jokes.explanation: 'Karmopolis' is a nonsense word delivered as if it's a perfectly normal surname. Its absurdity is the joke — Lucille's alias is not remotely convincing. Rewatch: on subsequent views, you notice Michael uses it to identify which room they're in.

jokes.text: Tobias (as George Sr.) slaps a jar out of Lindsay's hand: 'You don't need the calories.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is channeling George Sr.'s 'raw power' and returns home changed
jokes.punchline: His expression of masculine authority is slapping food out of Lindsay's hands and commenting on her calories — this is George Sr.-style cruelty expressed through Tobias
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Tobias, who is usually bumbling and inept, adopts George Sr.'s cruelty as 'power' — and Lindsay finds this ATTRACTIVE. The inverted dynamic (Tobias being harsh = appealing) is both funny and sharp commentary on the Bluth family's dysfunction. George Sr.'s specific cruelty about appearances maps back to the nose job joke earlier.

jokes.text: Lindsay whispering to herself: 'This isn't working.' / Then to Ann: 'Is it my imagination, or is he more commanding and mannish? It's like — I don't know. Maybe I do want to be with him.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has adopted George Sr.'s aggressive persona and Lindsay was initially dismissive
jokes.punchline: Lindsay is now attracted to Tobias BECAUSE he's acting like her father — the Freudian horror is played straight as Lindsay discovers this about herself in real time
jokes.explanation: This pays off Michael's earlier observation that Lindsay is 'still looking for somebody like Daddy.' The Freudian incest-adjacent joke is uncomfortable and deeply committed — Lindsay figuring out in real time that she's attracted to her husband-playing-her-father is peak Arrested Development dark comedy. The callback makes it richer.

jokes.text: Ann advises Lindsay to go to Tobias: 'For as it is written, you shall be with whom you have formed a more perfect union with under God.' / Lindsay: '[Laughs] She's right.'
jokes.setup: Ann gives Lindsay relationship advice in the form of quasi-Biblical scripture
jokes.punchline: The 'scripture' Ann cites sounds plausible but is clearly made up — and Lindsay, who was just reflecting on being attracted to her father-surrogate husband, takes it as wisdom
jokes.explanation: Ann's faux-Biblical proclamation is funny precisely because it's so vague and Lindsay accepts it completely. The irony of Ann, a devout Christian, advising someone to go be with their husband who is currently acting like that person's father is darkly comedic without Ann knowing it.

jokes.text: Tobias (still in George Sr. mode) rejects Lindsay: 'This isn't working.' / 'I'm sorry. You've got the wrong Motherboy.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay, now attracted to the dominant Tobias, comes to him
jokes.punchline: 'You've got the wrong Motherboy' — using the episode's title/concept as a rejection line, implying Tobias has now overcorrected into a persona that doesn't want Lindsay either
jokes.explanation: 'You've got the wrong Motherboy' is a multi-layered line: it uses the episode title as a punchline, suggests Tobias has become so George Sr. that he's lost interest in Lindsay (just as George Sr. was 'dismissive and disinterested'), and sets up the next scene at the actual Motherboy event. Dense and economical.

jokes.text: Michael asks if Lucille Bluth checked in; the desk clerk says no — Michael: 'She knows you're after her.' / Buster: 'She does?'
jokes.setup: Michael can't find Lucille under her real name
jokes.punchline: Buster's alarmed 'She does?' — as if Michael has revealed something terrifying about his mother's awareness — captures Buster's fear of Lucille perfectly
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat for Buster — his immediate terror at 'she knows you're after her' is funny because it implies Lucille is an apex predator and Buster is fundamentally afraid of her. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Michael and Buster mistake another mother-son pair in matching outfits and try to rescue the boy: 'Take me with you! No, please! Please!' / 'It's the wrong child.'
jokes.setup: Michael recognizes matching outfits and assumes it must be Lucille and George Michael
jokes.punchline: It's a completely different pair — and the boy BEGS to be taken anyway, suggesting he's equally miserable at Motherboy
jokes.explanation: The boy desperately begging 'Take me with you!' after the mistake is the best part — it's a brief moment that implies the entire Motherboy complex is full of suffering boys. It's quick but lands hard. The visual beat of dramatically rescuing the wrong child is solid physical comedy.

jokes.text: Buster explains Lucille's changing behavior: 'Whenever she changed clothes, she made me wait on the balcony until zip-up.' / 'And yet, anything goes at bath time.'
jokes.setup: Buster says if Lucille changed clothes, George Michael would be on the balcony
jokes.punchline: 'And yet, anything goes at bath time' — Buster cheerfully volunteers information that implies Lucille's modesty is completely inconsistent in a deeply disturbing way
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. 'And yet, anything goes at bath time' is delivered by Buster as a cheerful, offhand observation — the horror of what he's casually implying about his relationship with Lucille is maximized by his complete comfort with it. It's the perfect Arrested Development dark joke: the character has no idea how disturbing what they're saying is, which makes it exponentially funnier and more uncomfortable. High quotability, deeply character-specific to the Buster/Lucille dynamic, and the economy is perfect — seven words that destroy the audience.

jokes.text: Buster: '...And yet, anything goes at bath time.' [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Buster explains Lucille's strict costume-change modesty rules
jokes.punchline: 'Anything goes at bath time' — delivered with a nostalgic chuckle, implying the bath time rules were... different. Horrifyingly different.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's crown jewel. Six words that are somehow both the funniest and most disturbing thing in the episode. The structure is perfect: the setup (strict modesty rules during changes) makes the 'anything goes at bath time' punchline land as a complete character revelation. Buster's nostalgic chuckle makes it exponentially darker. Economy is absolute — not a single word wasted. This is comparable to the 9.5 CRAFT anchor; it's a cultural reference for AD fans, specific only to Buster, and the implication is maximally dark while never stating anything explicit. Among the best jokes the show ever produced.

jokes.text: Barry to Gob: 'Your job is to convince that "wife" of yours...' — he uses air quotes around 'wife'
jokes.setup: Barry is coaching Gob on how to handle the wife he doesn't remember
jokes.punchline: Barry's air quotes around 'wife' — even the lawyer doesn't believe the marriage is real
jokes.explanation: A quick beat — Barry's air quotes signal his own skepticism. Works as character color for Barry (the world's worst lawyer) but isn't a standout joke.

jokes.text: Gob: 'She has a name, Barry. You don't happen to know what it is by the way, do you?'
jokes.setup: Barry calls Gob's wife 'that wife of yours'
jokes.punchline: Gob defends her dignity by insisting she has a name — then immediately reveals he also doesn't know it
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured joke: Gob takes the moral high ground ('She has a name, Barry') and immediately undercuts himself by also not knowing it. The pivot is instant and perfectly delivered. Very Gob — he has principles he immediately violates. High quotability.

jokes.text: Barry: 'That's why I want to settle. I'm not super prepared.'
jokes.setup: Barry is supposedly Gob's legal counsel
jokes.punchline: Barry admits his motivation for settling is that he's 'not super prepared' — a stunning admission of legal incompetence delivered as a business strategy
jokes.explanation: Barry's incompetence is a running gag throughout the series, and 'I'm not super prepared' as a legal strategy is a perfect encapsulation of it. The matter-of-fact delivery of professional negligence is very funny.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, we did have sex. And I'm not a great liar.' / Narrator: 'Both things he just said were lies.'
jokes.setup: Gob insists they had sex and that he can't lie convincingly
jokes.punchline: The narrator authoritatively confirms BOTH statements are false — Gob is lying about the sex AND lying about not being a good liar
jokes.explanation: The narrator as lie detector — 'Both things he just said were lies' — is structurally brilliant. The twist is that Gob's denial of being a good liar is itself a lie, which is a logical paradox deployed as a punchline. The economy is excellent: six words do enormous work. Gob's dishonesty being exposed in real time by an omniscient narrator is a perfect use of the show's format.

jokes.text: Barry: 'And you don't want to go in front of that judge. I caught him in a drag club.' / Gob: 'What were you doin' there?' / Barry: 'Wow. You should be the lawyer.'
jokes.setup: Barry warns against going in front of the judge because he caught him in a drag club
jokes.punchline: Gob's logical follow-up question — 'What were you doing there?' — turns the tables on Barry, and Barry's admission that Gob has sharp instincts ('You should be the lawyer') confirms Barry's own guilt by implication
jokes.explanation: A classically structured exchange where the straight question deflates the authority of the person asking. Barry's 'You should be the lawyer' is the punchline that confirms: Barry was also at the drag club. The structure is clean and the inversion (incompetent lawyer praising the client's instincts) is perfect.

jokes.text: Michael spots a 'very bored-looking little Sonny Bono on that balcony' — meaning George Michael in the sailor suit variant.
jokes.setup: Michael and Buster are scanning balconies for George Michael
jokes.punchline: George Michael is described as 'a very bored-looking little Sonny Bono' — the visual of a teenager in a bad costume looking miserable on a balcony
jokes.explanation: The Sonny Bono costume detail is funny both visually and because of the implication: Lucille is dressed as Cher, making George Michael her Sonny — a mother-son relationship played as a romantic duo that mirrors the show's central Motherboy critique. Good layered visual gag.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I have an idea. It is time I used something I trained for in army, but I never got a chance to do.' / Michael: 'You cannot zip-line over there!'
jokes.setup: Buster gravely announces military training he can finally use
jokes.punchline: It's zip-lining — Michael's resigned 'You cannot zip-line over there!' confirms this is Buster's solemnly held tactical skill
jokes.explanation: The buildup (solemn military training, dramatic announcement) deflated by 'zip-lining' is a great misdirection. Buster's army training being entirely recreational activities is a perfect character joke. Michael's reaction completes it.

jokes.text: 'Either I zip down or he zips up! And that is a mighty long zipper on Mother's Cher jumpsuit. You have to get on your knees to start it.'
jokes.setup: The standoff about whether to zip-line
jokes.punchline: Buster's detailed knowledge of the length of Lucille's Cher jumpsuit zipper and the specific posture required to start it implies he has helped Lucille into this costume before — the intimacy of this information is the joke
jokes.explanation: Buster's intimate knowledge of his mother's wardrobe — specifically that you have to get on your knees to zip her Cher jumpsuit — is darkly funny. It implies a level of physical intimacy with his mother that is consistent with the Motherboy theme. 'A mighty long zipper' as a tactical assessment is also absurd. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Buster zip-lines across the hotel gap, screaming — crashes — and Lucille immediately calls 'Zip me up.' from offscreen
jokes.setup: Buster zip-lines to George Michael's rescue
jokes.punchline: He lands (presumably crashing) and immediately hears Lucille's 'Zip me up' — she is in the room, using her own son's rescue mission to get dressed
jokes.explanation: The 'Zip me up' callback from the opening of the episode lands here as a perfect structural callback — Buster's daring rescue mission immediately turns into serving Lucille's wardrobe needs. Lucille treating a zip-line rescue as a convenient wardrobe opportunity is peak character comedy. The physical slapstick of the zip-line plus the callback makes this a multi-layer joke.

jokes.text: 'Then what the hell am I wearin' these for? Look at that.' / [Tobias appears to be wearing cutoffs under his pants] / Tobias: 'It's a thing. There's dozens of us.'
jokes.setup: An actor on the Scandal Makers set realizes the character he's playing is straight, questioning why he's wearing something (implied: cutoffs)
jokes.punchline: Tobias explains that wearing cutoffs under pants is 'a thing' and 'there's dozens of us' — acknowledging his habit while implying there's a community of cutoff-wearers
jokes.explanation: 'There's dozens of us' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode and has become a cultural reference for niche communities. It plays as Tobias defending his never-nude lifestyle while inadvertently confirming how unusual it is. The 'dozens of us' framing (not thousands, not millions — dozens) perfectly calibrates the absurdity. Exceptional economy and quotability.

jokes.text: 'If that man's straight, then I am sober.'
jokes.setup: The actor is told the character he's playing (based on Tobias) is straight
jokes.punchline: The character's response uses the 'if X then I am Y' construction where Y (sober) implies he's not sober — and the subtext is clearly that Tobias is not straight
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical one-liner that does double duty: jokes about the actor's drinking AND Tobias's sexuality. A classic construction executed well, though not especially original.

jokes.text: 'Hi. Um, I'm waiting for "Gawb" Bluth.' / 'I'm Gob Bluth. My wife send you?' / 'I'm your wife.' / 'I knew that.' / 'Nice to see you again, Usarmy.'
jokes.setup: Gob is waiting for his wife in the legal context
jokes.punchline: His wife doesn't know how to pronounce his name ('Gawb'), he doesn't recognize her face, and he immediately overcorrects with 'I knew that' — then calls her 'Usarmy' (misremembering her name as something military-sounding)
jokes.explanation: The cascade of mutual ignorance is perfectly structured: she can't say his name, he doesn't know her face, he covers ('I knew that'), then reveals he thinks her name is 'Usarmy.' Three jokes in four lines. The specificity of 'Usarmy' (presumably a misread of her background rather than her name) is excellent. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Did I get lines? God! They told me I was just jumping onto a moving stair car.'
jokes.setup: Carl tells Tobias to work on his lines
jokes.punchline: Tobias is surprised and delighted to have lines — he thought his role was just physical business on a stair car (a callback to the Bluth stair car from the show's history)
jokes.explanation: The stair car callback is an AD signature. Tobias's excitement about having lines after assuming a purely physical role is good character comedy. Rewards rewatchers who know the stair car's history.

jokes.text: 'You lost a lot of weight in Iraq, especially up in the northern region.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to connect with his wife he doesn't know
jokes.punchline: 'Especially up in the northern region' — Gob compliments his wife's weight loss in a specific geographic area of her body using geopolitical terminology
jokes.explanation: 'Especially up in the northern region' is a wonderful line — Gob complimenting her body using a military/geographic metaphor (Iraq, northern region) that renders the compliment completely absurd. It also confirms he thinks her name is Usarmy (i.e., she served in Iraq). The obliviousness is total. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You lost a lot of weight in Iraq, especially up in the northern region.'
jokes.setup: Gob greets his wife after the long period since their one-night marriage
jokes.punchline: He compliments her weight loss in a war zone — 'especially up in the northern region' — trying to be suave while revealing he still doesn't remember or recognize her
jokes.explanation: 'Especially up in the northern region' as an attempt to compliment specific body parts while having no information is good Gob character work. He's improvising compliments based on where she served.

jokes.text: Gob: 'As far as I'm concerned, we were never even married.' / Wife: 'Maybe I'll just say that we never sealed the deal.' / Gob: 'You're willing to admit that?' / Wife: 'Lie about it? Yes.'
jokes.setup: The negotiation over whether to admit they didn't consummate the marriage
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe I'll just say that we never sealed the deal' — 'sealed' is both a legal term (sealed the deal = consummated) AND a reference to the seal that ate Buster's hand, which Gob released from his wife's zoo
jokes.explanation: 'Never sealed the deal' as a double pun — consummation AND the actual seal animal — is a beautiful piece of wordplay that rewards attention. The pun works on both a linguistic level and as a thematic callback to the seal subplot. This is Arrested Development at its densest and most rewarding.

jokes.text: 'Wow. I mis-underestimated you. You've got some courage. Makes me wonder what I'm missing.' / Gob: 'You mean, other than your cans?' / Wife: 'No. They're still here.'
jokes.setup: Gob's wife impresses him with her willingness to lie in court
jokes.punchline: The 'cans' callback from Gob's earlier obsession with her body pays off — he asks if she means 'other than your cans' and she confirms they're still there, breaking the tension with an absurd body check
jokes.explanation: A callback to the 'big cans' conversation from earlier. The wife's matter-of-fact 'No. They're still here' as a response to a serious moment is a great deflation, and 'mis-underestimated' (a malapropism of 'underestimated') is a nice bonus Gob-specific verbal slip.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You mean, other than your cans?' / Wife: 'No. They're still here.'
jokes.setup: Wife says she wonders what she's missing about Gob
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets 'what I'm missing' as a reference to her physical attributes (calling back his earlier obsession with her 'cans'); her literal response 'No. They're still here' confirms she understood his meaning and refutes it matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Clean callback to the earlier 'cans' conversation. The deadpan 'No. They're still here' is excellent — she doesn't react with outrage, just factual correction. Good character economy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And so Gob set about consummating the marriage he'd finally agreed to claim he hadn't.'
jokes.setup: Gob and his wife have agreed to claim the marriage was never consummated
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately proceeds to consummate it, making the legal strategy instantly false in the most Gob way possible
jokes.explanation: The narrator's phrasing is exceptional — the paradox is in the sentence structure itself: 'consummating the marriage he'd agreed to claim he hadn't.' This is both a character revelation (Gob immediately undoes his own legal strategy via impulsivity) and a beautiful piece of prose comedy. Strong economy and structure.

jokes.text: 'And back at Motherboy XXX, Lucille was finding her new partner an asset.' / Lucille to the judges: 'Is this your son?' / Lucille: 'No, I'm his grandmother. He's an orphan.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is competing in Motherboy with George Michael
jokes.punchline: To make them seem like a more sympathetic pair (and to explain the age gap), Lucille claims she's his GRANDMOTHER and that he's an ORPHAN — a double lie delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: Lucille's competitive ruthlessness is on full display — she invents a tragic backstory for George Michael to game the sympathy of the judges. 'He's an orphan' delivered with practiced ease is a great character beat. The narrator's 'finding her new partner an asset' also has an implied meaning given the 'asset/ass-et' possible wordplay in context.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I'm not an orphan. I have a dad.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has told the judges George Michael is an orphan
jokes.punchline: George Michael, sweet and literal, corrects the record — completely undermining Lucille's strategy with guileless honesty
jokes.explanation: George Michael's characteristic guilelessness is the joke — he is incapable of going along with Lucille's scheme because he's too sincere. 'I have a dad' is delivered with completely unnecessary clarification energy. Good character beat.

jokes.text: Lucille to George Michael after the judges leave: 'Well, that little bit of honesty helped nothing. You'd better bring it to the waltz, young man.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's correction cost them the sympathy angle
jokes.punchline: Lucille treats George Michael's honesty as a tactical mistake and pivots immediately to waltz performance — the absurdity of treating a dance competition with this gravity
jokes.explanation: 'You'd better bring it to the waltz, young man' — Lucille's total pivot to athletic coaching after a scheme collapses is perfect. She doesn't register the moral problem with lying to judges; she registers only the tactical failure. Pure Lucille.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Gypsies.' [seeing Lucille and George Michael in gypsy costumes]
jokes.setup: Michael and Buster spot Lucille and George Michael who have changed costumes
jokes.punchline: Buster's one-word delivery — 'Gypsies.' — as a flat, resigned observation about his mother's new costume choice is perfectly understated
jokes.explanation: A single-word reaction that conveys everything: Buster knows his mother, has probably been through this before, and is entirely unsurprised by 'gypsies.' The flat delivery of an absurd observation is peak Buster. Economy score is near-perfect — one word does the entire job.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I create a diversion, and you grab George Michael and go. We need a name.' / Buster: 'Maybe "Operation Hot Mother."' / Narrator: 'No, let's try to top that.' / Narrator: 'They never did.'
jokes.setup: Michael proposes a rescue operation that needs a name
jokes.punchline: Buster suggests 'Operation Hot Mother' — which is both a tactical name and an assessment of his own mother — and the narrator's 'They never did' confirms they went with it
jokes.explanation: This is tied for the best joke in the episode. 'Operation Hot Mother' is already absurd and darkly funny coming from Buster — calling his mother 'hot' in the context of a rescue mission. But the narrator's structure is perfect: 'No, let's try to top that' (inviting improvement) followed immediately by 'They never did' (confirming they went with the Oedipal name) is a masterful structural joke. The economy is exceptional. The narrator as collaborator and then straight-man delivers the punchline of 'They never did' with devastating efficiency. One of the show's best narrator gags.

jokes.text: Buster gets his hook caught on a sandbag during Operation Hot Mother and is hoisted into the air, whimpering, while the Motherboy announcer covers: 'Our curtain seems to be stuck. We're going to do some freestyle waltzing while we get it fixed.'
jokes.setup: Buster is supposed to cut the lights as Michael grabs George Michael
jokes.punchline: Buster's hook catches a sandbag and hoists him into the air — the MC covers with 'freestyle waltzing' while a one-armed man whimpers suspended above the event
jokes.explanation: Excellent physical comedy: Buster's hook — his disability — becomes the literal obstacle to his rescue mission. The MC's cover ('freestyle waltzing') transforms a disaster into a feature with perfect establishment tone. The image of Buster suspended and whimpering while the dance continues below is a great visual. Rewatch bonus for the image.

jokes.text: George Michael to Michael: 'They haven't served dinner yet.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to rescue George Michael from Motherboy
jokes.punchline: George Michael's hesitation is that dinner hasn't been served — a completely mundane concern in the middle of a dramatic rescue
jokes.explanation: A perfectly timed deadpan — George Michael's practical concern about missing dinner while being dramatically 'rescued' from a mother-son dance is funny because of the tonal collision. It also characterizes George Michael as someone who finds Motherboy more acceptable than his father does, which is a nice irony.

jokes.text: 'All right, Motherboys. Our curtain seems to be stuck. We're going to do some freestyle waltzing while we get it fixed.'
jokes.setup: The Motherboy curtain has collapsed due to Buster's hook incident
jokes.punchline: The emcee's calm, professional pivot to 'freestyle waltzing' treats the chaotic collapse of the event as a minor technical hiccup
jokes.explanation: The professional calm of the emcee during a catastrophe is reliable comedy. 'Freestyle waltzing' as an improvised event is a good absurdist detail. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael to Buster: 'I thought that you would be a little different, but you're not. I was wrong, okay? I'm sorry. You know, you're as good a brother as you ever were.'
jokes.setup: Michael confesses he avoided Buster because he was unsure how to treat him after the amputation
jokes.punchline: 'You're as good a brother as you ever were' — the intended compliment is undercut by 'as you ever were,' which is not a high bar given Buster's baseline
jokes.explanation: The moment is primarily emotional but has a comedic undercurrent: 'as good a brother as you ever were' sets the bar comically low. It's a touching scene with a gentle joke embedded in it. The cringe of Michael's accidental awkwardness is characteristic.

jokes.text: Buster, moved: '[Groaning] Thank you.' / 'I am no different. I am the same old Motherboy.'
jokes.setup: Buster is touched by Michael's apology
jokes.punchline: Buster calls himself 'the same old Motherboy' — using the event's name as his own identity, which is both sweet and horrifying given what Motherboy represents about his relationship with Lucille
jokes.explanation: 'I am the same old Motherboy' is a great line because Buster means it as reassurance but it's actually an admission that he's permanently defined by his enmeshed relationship with his mother. The humor is in his unconscious honesty. Rewatch: the episode title becomes a character description.

jokes.text: Buster announces: 'Boys' choice? That is, if you're not embarrassed to take my hook.'
jokes.setup: The MC calls 'boys' choice' for a waltz at Motherboy; Buster is suspended from the rigging
jokes.punchline: Buster asks if Michael is 'embarrassed to take my hook' — offering his prosthetic as a dance partner's hand with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: The sincerity of Buster offering his hook as a dance hand is moving and funny simultaneously — the show balancing pathos and comedy in Buster's disability without making him simply a punchline. 'Take my hook' for a waltz is both absurd and touching.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Boys' choice? That is, if you're not embarrassed to take my hook.' — leading George Michael in a waltz
jokes.setup: The freestyle waltz begins; Buster addresses George Michael
jokes.punchline: The invitation to dance via his hook — 'if you're not embarrassed to take my hook' — is vulnerable and dark but George Michael accepts without hesitation
jokes.explanation: More moving than funny, but the Peter Pan/Captain Hook framing that follows makes it a successful dark comedy beat. The acceptance of the hook as a dance partner is the episode's emotional resolution delivered through comedy.

jokes.text: 'I'm Peter Pan, and you're Captain Hook. We'll pretend it's a costume. Let's dance.'
jokes.setup: Michael agrees to dance with Buster and his hook at Motherboy
jokes.punchline: Michael's solution to the awkwardness of dancing with his hook-handed brother is to reframe it as a Peter Pan/Captain Hook costume — turning disability into theater
jokes.explanation: A genuinely sweet moment with comedic texture: Michael's impulse is to manage the disability narrative by reframing it as costume play. The Peter Pan/Captain Hook dynamic also has a meta resonance given the Lucille/Buster dynamic — Buster never grew up (Peter Pan is the one who does), so Michael claiming Peter Pan for himself is a small character statement. Rewatch bonus for the thematic layers.

jokes.text: Michael, Buster, and George Michael arrive at the Promise Land — Buster holds a 'Saddest' trophy
jokes.setup: The three arrive at the Promise Land camping trip feeling good about themselves
jokes.punchline: Buster is apparently holding a 'Saddest' trophy — they won a category at Motherboy, just not Cutest Couple — the 'Saddest' award being the most accurate possible description of their situation
jokes.explanation: The 'Saddest' trophy is an exceptional visual gag payoff: it's the perfect summation of the Bluth family's entire self-perception gap. They feel victorious; they won 'Saddest.' The category itself is a devastating and precise joke about the Bluths' obliviousness. Lines like 'And I finally feel good about myself. Saddest.' and 'That was a very competitive category' heighten it. High impact score because the reveal lands as both funny and thematically resonant.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That was a very competitive category.' / Buster: 'Oh, don't I know it.'
jokes.setup: They've just won the 'Saddest' category at Motherboy
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and Buster discuss the competitiveness of the 'Saddest' category with complete sincerity — the implication being there was stiff competition for most pathetic
jokes.explanation: The callback to the trophy is deepened by both of them discussing the competitiveness of 'Saddest' with total pride. Buster's 'Oh, don't I know it' is particularly rich — of course Buster, who lost a hand to a seal, is the expert on the 'Saddest' category. One of the episode's most rewatchable small moments.

jokes.text: Buster can't get out of the car: 'I can't get out, actually.'
jokes.setup: They've arrived and are getting out of the car
jokes.punchline: Buster cheerfully announces he's stuck in the car — implying the hook has caught on something — with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: A brief tag on the hook running gag — consistent with Buster's hook catching on things throughout the episode. Simple but well-timed.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development' tag: Lindsay: 'I have never been more attracted to you.' / Tobias: 'Wow. The service sent you over fast.' / Lindsay: 'Oh, God. I'm so sorry. I'm in the wrong trailer.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she's never been more attracted to him
jokes.punchline: Tobias thinks she was sent by 'the service' — meaning he called an escort — and Lindsay realizes she's in the wrong trailer (meaning she accidentally went to the actor playing Tobias rather than Tobias himself)
jokes.explanation: The next-episode teaser is a joke itself: Lindsay's attraction is to someone she thinks is Tobias-but-dominant, Tobias thinks an escort arrived, and Lindsay is in the WRONG TRAILER — meaning she's attracted to someone playing Tobias, not Tobias. Three levels of mistaken identity compressed into one tag. The show is rich enough to pack this quality into a promo.

jokes.text: Gob's 'lucky break' in the 'on the next': Judge looks at compromising photo and says 'There's no way to tell who this man is.' / Then Gob: 'That's me, Your Honor. I — [bleep] my wife.' / Barry: 'We really lost this case.'
jokes.setup: Gob is about to benefit from an unidentifiable compromising photo of the judge
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately and voluntarily identifies himself IN the photo to the judge and confesses to having sex with his wife — destroying his own case — while Barry summarizes: 'We really lost this case'
jokes.explanation: Gob self-destructing his legal case by voluntarily identifying himself in an incriminating photo — when the judge literally said there was no way to tell who it was — is perfect Gob character comedy. 'We really lost this case' from Barry is an excellent deadpan summary. The sequence is structurally tight: setup (photo unidentifiable) → Gob destroys it → Barry's epitaph. High quotability.

jokes.text: Maeby tells a campfire horror story: 'Knock, scrape. The only thing more terrifying than the escaped lunatic's hook was his twisted call!' / Buster appears: 'Hey, campers.' / Kids scream. / 'I'm a monster!'
jokes.setup: Maeby tells a campfire story about an escaped lunatic with a hook
jokes.punchline: Buster — the man with an actual hook for a hand — shows up at the campfire saying 'Hey, campers,' causing children to scream; Buster then screams 'I'm a monster!' in despair
jokes.explanation: This is the single best joke in the episode. The classic horror-story trope of the hook-handed escaped lunatic pays off with perfect timing when the actual hook-handed man shows up innocently saying 'Hey, campers.' Then Buster's 'I'm a monster!' — his realization that he IS the campfire story — is one of the most perfectly constructed comedy beats in the series. Structure is near-flawless: setup (horror story), subversion (innocent Buster), escalation (kids screaming), punchline (Buster's self-recognition). Character integration is total — only Buster, with his hook, his depression, his child-like nature, and his damaged self-image, could deliver 'I'm a monster!' with this kind of devastating impact. This is a 9+ calibration moment.

tmdb_overview: Lucille abducts George Michael for a mother-son dance, Buster adjusts to having a hook for a hand and Gob reunites with his estranged wife. Also, Tobias stars as George Sr. in a biopic about the Bluth family, which causes Lindsay to become more attracted to him.
insight: Motherboy pageant spawns 58 jokes in 58 minutes—character-driven chaos at near-perfect density.
